worked: **The recurring lesson (now atoned S3):** I asserted "the daemon consumes the queue" in a handoff without reading the consumer (the daemon source isn't even on this machine). schema-parity ≠ consumption-proof. Verified the cheap claim, asserted the load-bearing one. Caught by the i-dream agent, not by me or my own review (which grounds _code_, not _cross-system architectural premises_ — a real boundary of the review system). Precheck recorded: before writing "X consumes/reads/produces Y", grep for the actual reader; if absent, mark UNVERIFIED, never assert.
**"Fix the class, not the named instances":** review pass 1 named 3 leaking builders; I fixed those 3, pass 2 found a 4th (`build-proposals.sh`). Grep the whole class.

**Reviewer safety gap (fixed):** the skeptical-review agent mutated a live store (`guidance.sh add` against the real notes file) while testing. Skill now mandates read-only + /tmp-copy testing. No real data lost (file had no real directives yet).

**User preferences reaffirmed:** wants real teeth not theater; prose mistakes can stay forensic, code mistakes are the priority; terse/qualitative comments; a neutral guidance channel read by the agent AND all homework-checkers; records atone to prevent recurrence.

**Gotcha:** the Bash tool render corrupts the string "ingest-queue" → "ln" in some grep output — trust Read/ls over grep for that path. bash 3.2 chokes on heredoc-inside-`$()`; use Python subprocess list-args for quote-heavy CLI calls.
